target.name: khayanolide-type limonoids tetracyclic core framework
target.motivation: Khayanolide-type limonoids, such as krishnolides A and C, possess complex polycyclic scaffolds and exhibit significant biological activities, including anti-HIV activity, making them compelling targets for total synthesis.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Construction of the unique methanoindene cage structure
Construction of the unique methanoindene cage structure

success_criteria.free_text: Enantioselective construction of an advanced intermediate containing the [5,5,6,6]-tetracyclic core framework with precise stereochemical fidelity.

key_disconnections.bond_description: A2 ring and C30 hydroxy group
key_disconnections.reasoning: To form the A2 ring and simultaneously install the hydroxy group at C30 from tetracycle 10.

key_disconnections.bond_description: B ring with all-cis stereochemical configuration, quaternary carbon at C10, and tertiary alcohol at C1
key_disconnections.reasoning: To establish the B ring with the desired stereochemistry from dienone 11.
To introduce the D ring from acetate 12.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy aims for a convergent synthesis of the tetracyclic core framework of khayanolide-type limonoids. It leverages an acylative kinetic resolution for enantioselectivity, a 1,2-Grignard addition for fragment coupling, and an AcOH-interrupted Nazarov cyclization to establish the key tetracyclic system with precise stereochemical control.

thought.background: Krishnolides A and C (7 and 8) are complex khayanolide-type limonoids with a structurally intricate tricyclo[4.2.1^10,30.1^1,4]decane ring system and multiple stereogenic centers. Previous syntheses focused on the methanoindene cage. The current goal is to synthesize the [5,5,6,6]-tetracyclic core framework.
thought.gap: How to efficiently and enantioselectively construct the [5,5,6,6]-tetracyclic core framework, specifically the A2 ring, B ring, and D ring, with correct stereochemistry.
thought.decision: To synthesize krishnolides A and C from diol 9 via late-stage modification. Diol 9 would come from tetracycle 10 via intramolecular pinacol coupling. Tetracycle 10 would be derived from dienone 11 via an AcOH-interrupted Nazarov cyclization. Dienone 11 would be formed from acetate 12 via intramolecular aldol condensation. Acetate 12 would be prepared from aldehyde 14 and alpha-iodoenone 13 via 1,2-Grignard addition.

evidence.text_span: Our retrosynthetic analysis toward krishnolides A (7) and C (8) is delineated in Scheme 1B. We hypothesized that these two molecules could be synthesized from diol 9 through a late-stage modification involving adjustment of the oxidation state and regioselective acylation. The formation of 9 was envisioned to proceed via an intramolecular pinacol coupling [30,31] of [5,5,6,6]-tetracycle 10, which forges the A2 ring while simultaneously installing the hydroxy group at C30. The latter intermediate could in turn be derived from dienone 11 by an AcOHinterrupted Nazarov cyclization [32-34], thereby establishing the B ring with the desired all-cis stereochemical configuration, including the quaternary carbon at C10 and the essential tertiary alcohol at C1. The β-hydroxylactone moiety (D ring) in 11 could be introduced through an intramolecular aldol condensation [35] of acetate 12. Ultimately, the preparation of 12 could be traced back to aldehyde 14 through 1,2-Grignard addition with an organomagnesium reagent [36] prepared from α-iodoenone 13.

thought.background: The synthesis of alpha-iodoenone 13 is required. A known method for alpha-monomethylation of cyclohexenone exists.
thought.gap: How to efficiently methylate cyclohexenone 15 at the alpha position.
thought.decision: To perform alpha-monomethylation of cyclohexenone 15 using LDA, HMPA, and MeI to produce enone 16.

outcome.observation: Enone 16 was obtained in 78% yield.

evidence.text_span: Our synthesis began with the preparation of α-iodoenone 13 (Scheme 2). The α-monomethylation of cyclohexenone 15 was efficiently carried out with LDA, HMPA, and MeI [37], producing enone 16 in 78% yield.

thought.background: Enone 16 is available. A diastereoselective aldol reaction is needed to introduce the furaldehyde moiety.
thought.gap: How to achieve high regioselectivity and diastereoselectivity in the aldol reaction between 16 and 3-furaldehyde.
thought.decision: To use LiHMDS to promote the diastereoselective aldol reaction between 16 and 3-furaldehyde.

outcome.observation: Alcohol 17 was obtained in 71% yield with high regioselectivity (14:1 dr at C17).

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, a diastereoselective aldol reaction between 16 and 3-furaldehyde promoted by LiHMDS gave alcohol 17 with high regioselectivity (14:1 dr at C17) after an extensive screening of bases (LDA, NaHMDS, KHMDS, etc.).

thought.background: Alcohol 17 is available. Enantioselectivity is crucial for the overall synthesis. An acylative kinetic resolution is a known method for achieving this.
thought.gap: How to achieve satisfactory efficiency and enantioselectivity in the acylative kinetic resolution of alcohol 17.
thought.decision: To use (R)-BTM (19) for the acylative kinetic resolution of alcohol 17 to furnish acetate 18.

outcome.observation: Acetate 18 was obtained in 37% yield with 85% ee.

evidence.text_span: Drawing inspiration from the pioneering work of Birman [38], as well as Newhouse’s applications [18,19], an acylative kinetic resolution of the alcohol was achieved by using (R)-BTM (19), furnishing acetate 18 with satisfactory efficiency and enantioselectivity (37% yield, 85% ee).

thought.background: Acetate 18 is available. The final step for fragment 13 is iodination.
thought.gap: How to efficiently iodinate 18 to produce alpha-iodoenone 13.
thought.decision: To use Johnsen’s protocol (I2, pyridine) for the iodination of 18.

outcome.observation: Alpha-iodoenone 13 was obtained in 89% yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, iodination of 18 employing Johnsen’s protocol (I2, pyridine) [39] provided α-iodoenone 13 in 89% yield.

thought.background: The synthesis of acetal aldehyde 14 requires bicyclic ketone 21, which can be prepared from (+)-Hajos–Parrish ketone.
thought.gap: How to convert (+)-Hajos–Parrish ketone to bicyclic ketone 21.
thought.decision: To prepare bicyclic ketone 21 from (+)-Hajos–Parrish ketone in two steps.

outcome.observation: Bicyclic ketone 21 was prepared in 49% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, the synthesis of acetal aldehyde 14 commenced with bicyclic ketone 21, which was prepared from (+)-Hajos–Parrish ketone in 49% yield over two steps (Scheme 3) [40-43].

thought.background: Bicyclic ketone 21 is available. The next step is to introduce a methyl group at C10.
thought.gap: How to introduce the methyl group at C10 with good yield and diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To perform silyl enol etherification of ketone 21, followed by IBX-mediated Nicolaou oxidation to form the enone, and then a Michael addition with MeMgBr and CuI to introduce the methyl group at C10.

outcome.observation: Enone was formed in 72% yield (90% brsm) after oxidation. Michael addition afforded 22 in 65% yield with 4:1 dr at C10.

evidence.text_span: Ensuring silyl enol etherification of the ketone at C29 coupled with IBX-mediated Nicolaou oxidation [44] furnished the corresponding enone in 72% yield (90% brsm). The methyl group at C10 was then introduced via a Michael addition (MeMgBr, CuI) to afford 22 in a yield of 65% (4:1 dr at C10).

thought.background: Ketone 22 is available. The next step is to perform a carbonyl 1,2-transposition.
thought.gap: The reported carbonyl 1,2-transposition protocol by Dong and co-workers was considered.
thought.decision: To attempt the carbonyl 1,2-transposition protocol reported by Dong and co-workers.

outcome.observation: Initial attempts on the carbonyl 1,2-transposition protocol reported by Dong and co-workers were ineffective, leading to premature hydride termination and the formation of alkene 23.

evidence.text_span: Initial attempts on the carbonyl 1,2-transposition protocol reported by Dong and co-workers were ineffective [45], leading to premature hydride termination and the formation of alkene 23.

thought.background: The previous attempt at carbonyl 1,2-transposition failed. An alternative strategy is needed to form alkene 23.
thought.gap: How to convert ketone 22 to alkene 23 efficiently.
thought.decision: To treat 22 with KHMDS and PhNTf2 for enol triflation, followed by coupling the resultant triflate with n-Bu3SnH to afford Δ1,29-alkene 23.

outcome.observation: Enol triflation took place successfully, and the subsequent coupling afforded Δ1,29-alkene 23 in 83% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: As an alternative solution, by treating 22 with KHMDS and PhNTf2, enol triflation took place successfully. The resultant triflate was coupled with n-Bu3SnH to afford Δ1,29-alkene 23 in 83% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Alkene 23 is available. The next step is hydroboration–oxidation to introduce a hydroxyl group, followed by oxidation to ketone 24.
thought.gap: How to selectively convert the desired alcohol to ketone 24, given the formation of regioisomeric alcohols.
thought.decision: To perform hydroboration–oxidation using BH3·THF, followed by selective oxidation of the desired alcohol to ketone 24 using PCC. The undesired alcohol can be reverted to 21 by Swern oxidation.

outcome.observation: Hydroboration–oxidation proceeded smoothly, providing a 4.4:1 mixture of regioisomeric alcohols. The desired alcohol was selectively converted to ketone 24 using PCC, producing a 50% overall yield. The recovered undesired alcohol could be reverted to 21 by Swern oxidation.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent hydroboration–oxidation by employing BH3·THF proceeded smoothly, providing a 4.4:1 mixture of regioisomeric alcohols with the desired isomer being the major component, presumably due to the considerable steric hindrance from the quaternary carbon at C4. However, decagram-scale separation of these two isomers by chromatography proved troublesome. Fortunately, the distinct reactivity of those two alcohols toward oxidation allowed for the selective conversion of desired alcohol to ketone 24 using PCC, producing a 50% overall yield, while the recovered undesired alcohol could be reverted to 21 by Swern oxidation.

thought.background: Ketone 24 is available. The next step is enol triflation.
thought.gap: Direct enol triflation methods were considered.
thought.decision: To attempt direct enol triflation of 24 using various reagents (Et3N/Tf2O, NaH/PhNTf2, DTBMP/Tf2O).

outcome.observation: Direct enol triflation of 24 led only to epimerization at C10 or slow decomposition of the starting material.

evidence.text_span: Direct enol triflation (Et3N/Tf2O, NaH/PhNTf2, DTBMP/Tf2O, etc.) of 24 led only to epimerization at C10 or slow decomposition of the starting material (see Supporting Information File 1, Table S1).

thought.background: Direct enol triflation of 24 failed. An alternative approach is needed to form enol triflate 25.
thought.gap: How to regioselectively generate the TMS enol ether and then convert it to the enol triflate.
thought.decision: To treat 24 with HMDS and TMSI to generate the TMS enol ether, followed by Li/Si exchange with MeLi and interception of the lithium enolate with PhNTf2 to give enol triflate 25.

outcome.observation: Enol triflate 25 was obtained in 59% overall yield.

evidence.text_span: Pleasingly, treating 24 with HMDS and TMSI regioselectively generated the expected TMS enol ether [46], which underwent a Li/Si exchange in the presence of MeLi followed by interception of the lithium enolate with PhNTf2 to give enol triflate 25 in 59% overall yield.

thought.background: Enol triflate 25 is available. The next step is to introduce a methoxycarbonyl group.
thought.gap: How to perform methoxycarbonylation efficiently.
thought.decision: To use palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation to produce methyl ester 26.

outcome.observation: Methyl ester 26 was produced in a satisfactory yield of 84%.

evidence.text_span: The following palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation produced methyl ester 26 in a satisfactory yield of 84%.

thought.background: Methyl ester 26 is available. The goal is to prepare TIPS-protected allylic alcohol 28.
thought.gap: How to convert 26 to 28 through deketalization, reduction, and silylation.
thought.decision: To perform deketalization, reduction with DIBAL-H, and silylation of the primary alcohol with TIPSCl/Et3N to obtain 28.

outcome.observation: Allylic alcohol 27 was obtained in 62% yield after deketalization and reduction. TIPS-protected allylic alcohol 28 was obtained in 77% yield after silylation and oxidation.

evidence.text_span: TIPS-protected allylic alcohol 28 was selected as the appropriate precursor for the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde and synthesized from 26 via a four-step transformation sequence, including deketalization, reduction, silylation of the primary alcohol and oxidation of the secondary alcohol.

thought.background: TIPS-protected allylic alcohol 28 is available. The next step is to cleave the C2–C7 bond to deliver aldehyde 29.
thought.gap: How to perform the C2–C7 bond disconnection.
thought.decision: To apply a two-step protocol involving Rubottom oxidation (NaHMDS, TMSCl then m-CPBA) and PIDA-promoted oxidative cleavage to deliver aldehyde 29.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde 29 was delivered in 46% yield.

evidence.text_span: For the disconnection of the C2–C7 bond, a two-step protocol involving Rubottom oxidation and PIDA-promoted oxidative cleavage [47] was applied to deliver aldehyde 29.

thought.background: Aldehyde 29 is available. The final step for fragment 14 is to convert it to acetal aldehyde 14.
thought.gap: How to perform acetalization and desilylation, followed by oxidation to yield acetal aldehyde 14.
thought.decision: To perform a one-pot acetalization (HC(OMe)3, p-TsOH-H2O) and desilylation (TBAF), followed by oxidation with TPAP/NMO to furnish acetal aldehyde 14.

outcome.observation: Acetal aldehyde 14 was furnished in 67% yield over two steps (acetalization/desilylation and oxidation).

evidence.text_span: Finally, a one-pot acetalization and desilylation effectively afforded the acetal alcohol, which was then oxidized with the aid of TPAP, furnishing acetal aldehyde 14 in 67% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Fragments 13 and 14 are available. The next stage is the construction of the target skeleton through a 1,2-addition.
thought.gap: How to generate organometallic species from alpha-iodoenone 13 for 1,2-addition with aldehyde 14.
thought.decision: To attempt Li/I exchange under various conditions (n-BuLi, t-BuLi, or t-BuLi in combination with CeCl3 or MgBr2).

outcome.observation: Preliminary trials to generate organometallic species via Li/I exchange under various conditions led to rapid decomposition, likely due to inherent instability of alpha-iodoenone 13.

evidence.text_span: Preliminary trials to generate organometallic species via Li/I exchange under various conditions (n-BuLi, t-BuLi, or t-BuLi in combination with CeCl3 or MgBr2) led to rapid decomposition, likely due to inherent instability of α-iodoenone 13.

thought.background: Li/I exchange for alpha-iodoenone 13 failed due to decomposition. An alternative method for generating the Grignard reagent is needed.
thought.gap: How to generate a stable organometallic species from 13 for 1,2-addition.
thought.decision: To use Mg/I exchange with iPrMgCl·LiCl at -78 °C to generate the Grignard reagent, which would then react with aldehyde 14.

outcome.observation: The resulting Grignard reagent reacted smoothly with aldehyde 14, affording the corresponding adduct 12 in 58% yield (92% brsm).

evidence.text_span: Inspired by the influential studies by Knochel [36] and Baran [48], we discovered that Mg/I exchange of 13 could be accomplished with iPrMgCl·LiCl at −78 °C. The resulting Grignard reagent reacted smoothly with aldehyde 14, affording the corresponding adduct 12 in 58% yield (92% brsm).

thought.background: Adduct 12 is available. The next step is to form the beta-hydroxylactone and then dienone 11.
thought.gap: How to perform intramolecular aldol reaction and subsequent oxidation.
thought.decision: To treat 12 with LiHMDS to furnish the beta-hydroxylactone, which would then be converted to dienone 11 through TPAP oxidation.

outcome.observation: Beta-hydroxylactone was formed and converted to dienone 11 in 55% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Since the newly created configuration at C30 was inconsequential, an intramolecular aldol reaction was directly carried out by treatment with LiHMDS to furnish β-hydroxylactone, which could be converted to dienone 11 through TPAP oxidation.

thought.background: Dienone 11 is available. The pivotal Nazarov cyclization is the next step.
thought.gap: How to achieve the Nazarov cyclization of 11.
thought.decision: To attempt acid-mediated Nazarov conditions (AlCl3, BF3·Et2O, and Me2AlCl) and also AcOH.

outcome.observation: Initial trials under acid-mediated Nazarov conditions led to complete decomposition, while exposure to AcOH resulted in recovery of the starting material.

evidence.text_span: Initial trials under acid-mediated Nazarov conditions (AlCl3, BF3·Et2O and Me2AlCl) led to complete decomposition, while the exposure to AcOH resulted in recovery of the starting material (Table 1, entries 1–4).

thought.background: Acid-mediated Nazarov cyclization failed for dienone 11. Milder conditions are needed.
thought.gap: How to achieve Nazarov cyclization using photo-Nazarov conditions.
thought.decision: To attempt photo-Nazarov cyclization using UV-light at 365 nm or 313 nm.

outcome.observation: Irradiation at 365 or 313 nm failed to induce cyclization and only allowed recovery of the starting material.

evidence.text_span: While irradiation at 365 or 313 nm failed to induce cyclization and only allowed recovery of the starting material (Table 1, entries 6 and 7)

thought.background: Previous photo-Nazarov attempts at 365 nm and 313 nm failed. Different UV-light sources might be critical.
thought.gap: How to achieve photo-Nazarov cyclization of 11.
thought.decision: To attempt photo-Nazarov cyclization of 11 in the presence of AcOH by exposure to UV-light at 254 nm.

outcome.observation: The disrotatory cyclization of 11 occurred exclusively to provide an inseparable mixture of 32 and 33.

evidence.text_span: the disrotatory cyclization of 11 in the presence of AcOH by exposure to UV-light at 254 nm occurred exclusively to provide an inseparable mixture of 32 and 33 (Table 1, entry 5).

thought.background: An inseparable mixture of 32 and 33 was obtained from the photo-Nazarov cyclization. Dehydration is needed to obtain separable enones.
thought.gap: How to dehydrate the mixture of 32 and 33 to yield separable enones 34 and 10.
thought.decision: To perform dehydration of the resultant mixture with SOCl2 and pyridine.

outcome.observation: Separable enones 34 (51%) and 10 (9%) were yielded over two steps (Nazarov cyclization and dehydration). The structure of 10 was confirmed by X-ray crystallography.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent dehydration of the resultant mixture with SOCl2 and pyridine yielded separable enones 34 (51%) and 10 (9%) over two steps. The structure of 10 was unequivocally determined through X-ray crystallographic analysis (ORTEP drawing, Scheme 4).

thought.background: The photo-Nazarov cyclization of 11 yielded an inseparable mixture of 32 and 33. Optimization of reaction temperature was considered.
thought.gap: Whether elevating the reaction temperature would alter the ratio of 32 and 33.
thought.decision: To attempt photo-Nazarov cyclization at elevated temperatures.

outcome.observation: Further optimization by elevating the reaction temperature did not noticeably alter the ratio of 32 and 33.

evidence.text_span: Further optimization by elevating the reaction temperature did not noticeably alter the ratio of 32 and 33 (Table 1, entries 8 and 9).

thought.background: The photo-Nazarov cyclization of 11 yielded an inseparable mixture. Interrupted Nazarov cyclization with H2O under neutral conditions was considered.
thought.gap: Whether interrupted Nazarov cyclization with H2O under neutral conditions would be effective.
thought.decision: To attempt interrupted Nazarov cyclization with H2O under neutral conditions.

outcome.observation: Attempts to apply interrupted Nazarov cyclization with H2O under neutral conditions merely resulted in decomposition.

evidence.text_span: Moreover, attempts to apply interrupted Nazarov cyclization with H2O under neutral conditions merely resulted in decomposition (Table 1, entry 10).

thought.background: Previous Nazarov cyclization attempts on dienone 11 were problematic. A modified substrate might improve selectivity.
thought.gap: How to achieve a smooth cyclization and selective formation of 10 without 34.
thought.decision: To methylate dienone 11 to form methyl ether 31, and then perform photoirradiation at 254 nm in the presence of AcOH at 20 °C.

outcome.observation: Photoirradiation of the corresponding methyl ether 31 led to a smooth cyclization, followed by spontaneous elimination, which produced the desired 10 as the single product, with no detection of 34. Methyl ether 31 was obtained in 79% yield from 11.

evidence.text_span: To our delight, photoirradiation of the corresponding methyl ether 31 at 254 nm in the presence of AcOH at 20 °C led to a smooth cyclization, followed by spontaneous elimination, which produced the desired 10 as the single product, with no detection of 34 (Table 1, entry 11). This result presumably arises from the minimization of dipole–dipole repulsions between the carbonyl of the dienone moiety and the C14-OMe within the desired transition state (more details were discussed in our group’s previous work [29]).

validation.significance: The developed convergent approach successfully constructed the [5,5,6,6]-tetracyclic core framework of khayanolide-type limonoids enantioselectively. Key steps include an acylative kinetic resolution, a 1,2-Grignard addition, and an AcOH-interrupted Nazarov cyclization. This advanced intermediate (10) is a crucial precursor for the total synthesis of krishnolides A and C, and other khayanolide-type limonoids.

source.title: A convergent synthetic approach to the tetracyclic core framework of khayanolide-type limonoids.
source.authors: Zhang Z, Hu J, Ding H, Zhang L, Rao P.
